script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. Tuesday — here is your MEME recap. The portfolio closed six point one seven percent down on the session. Every primary holding finished in the red, led by a dramatic collapse in one name that demands immediate attention.

Sandisk. Down twelve point six three percent in a single session, shedding two hundred forty-one dollars and ninety-five cents to close at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents. That is not a routine pullback. That is sector-level dislocation, and the story behind it runs deeper than one stock.

No macro news was available for Tuesday's session. What is clear from the tape is that technology and semiconductor-adjacent names bore the brunt of the selling, with the Nasdaq composite posting a notable decline driven by escalating Middle East tensions following reports of a Trump administration blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices spiked on the news, pressuring high-multiple growth and AI infrastructure names across the board.

The top story of Tuesday's session belongs to Sandisk. The memory stock fell twelve point six three percent, its worst single-session performance in recent memory, closing at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents. The catalyst was a broad memory sector selloff tied to rising geopolitical risk. Reports indicate the Strait of Hormuz situation rattled semiconductor supply chain confidence, with SK Hynix shares falling more than five percent in South Korea in sympathy. Despite the carnage, Wall Street did not retreat. According to reporting from finance dot yahoo dot com, multiple analysts raised price targets on Sandisk, citing AI-driven supply tightness as a structural tailwind that Tuesday's macro shock did not fundamentally alter. One headline read plainly: analysts say buy the dip. The thesis rests on a memory boom potentially lasting longer than consensus expects. For this portfolio, Sandisk carries a five percent weight, and Tuesday's move erased meaningful ground. The analyst community's conviction, however, appears intact. The next move in this name will likely hinge on whether macro pressure stabilizes.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, largest weight first.

Applied Optoelectronics closed at one hundred eleven dollars and eighty-eight cents, down six point seven zero percent on Tuesday's session. No specific news drove this move in isolation. The stock's decline aligns with the broader technology selloff, particularly in optical networking and AI infrastructure hardware. Applied Optoelectronics remains the portfolio's largest position at seven point four three percent, so the drag here was material. Watch for any data center spending commentary that could reset expectations for optical component demand.

Redwire Corporation closed at nine dollars and fifty-nine cents, down five point eight zero percent on the session. No dedicated news crossed for Redwire on Tuesday, but the space sector broadly sold off following a reported milestone from China's rocket program, which rattled investor confidence in American commercial space dominance. That narrative pressure, combined with the oil spike, weighed on industrials with space exposure. Redwire carries a six point nine seven percent portfolio weight. The China space catalyst is a story worth monitoring closely.

AST SpaceMobile closed at sixty-seven dollars and fifty-eight cents, down seven point eight three percent on Tuesday's session. This was one of the sharper single-session declines in the portfolio. The same China rocket milestone that clipped Redwire hit AST SpaceMobile directly. According to finance dot yahoo dot com, a surprise achievement from China's space program cracked the Wall Street narrative around American rocket dominance, sending shockwaves through the sector already under pressure from spiking oil prices. SpaceX and Rocket Lab fell in the same move. AST SpaceMobile's satellite-to-smartphone business model carries a premium tied to American commercial space leadership, and that premium came under scrutiny on Tuesday. This is now the key overhang to monitor in this name.

Over at Nebius Group, the stock closed at two hundred ten dollars and fifty-one cents, down four point one six percent. A relevant item from Tuesday involves Google moving its tensor processing units deeper into neocloud providers, according to finance dot yahoo dot com. Nebius, as a fast-growing neocloud operator, operates directly in the path of that competitive dynamic. TPU availability within the neocloud ecosystem could shift the compute procurement calculus for companies like Nebius. This is an emerging story for this position.

Bloom Energy closed at two hundred thirty-three dollars and forty-nine cents, down four point five five percent on Tuesday's session. Despite the red close, the news flow around Bloom Energy was notably constructive. UBS maintained a buy rating on the stock, telling investors to buy the dip, according to finance dot yahoo dot com. The bank highlighted Bloom Energy's more than eight hundred percent gain over the trailing twelve months and framed the fuel cell maker as one of the central AI infrastructure stories in the current market. ClearBridge Investments separately cited alternative power demand as a key driver in their second-quarter commentary. The stock's premium valuation remains the primary debate, but institutional conviction appears high.

TeraWulf closed at twenty dollars and eighty-nine cents, down four point nine two percent on the session. Finance dot yahoo dot com published a comparative analysis of Applied Digital and TeraWulf, framing both as neocloud infrastructure plays helping ease the AI computing bottleneck. TeraWulf's positioning in that narrative remains intact. Tuesday's decline tracked the broader technology and crypto-adjacent selloff. Nothing in Tuesday's news flow appears to have altered the fundamental case.

Lumentum Holdings closed at seven hundred sixty-eight dollars and fifteen cents, down four point two two percent on Tuesday's session. No dedicated news was available for Lumentum. The optical components space tracked the broader technology weakness. With a five point five zero percent portfolio weight, the dollar move here was notable. Lumentum's next catalyst is likely tied to data center capex guidance from hyperscalers, which continues to shape demand expectations for optical networking hardware.

IREN Limited closed at thirty-eight dollars and ninety-eight cents, down five point two five percent on the session. No specific news was available for IREN on Tuesday. The stock's decline is consistent with the broader pressure on AI infrastructure and bitcoin mining-adjacent names. IREN carries a five percent portfolio weight. Watch for any commentary around power costs and energy availability, given the oil price move from Tuesday's geopolitical developments.

Sandisk, already covered in depth as the top story, closed at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents, down twelve point six three percent. The analyst community's response was constructive, but the macro environment will determine whether the memory trade finds footing in the sessions ahead.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve speaker comments on inflation and rate trajectory, given that the interest rate environment directly affects the high-multiple growth names concentrated in this portfolio. Second, oil price levels in response to the Strait of Hormuz situation — further escalation would extend pressure on space, semiconductor, and infrastructure names across the book. Third, any response or commentary from memory sector players, including SK Hynix, regarding supply chain exposure to the Middle East, which would directly inform the Sandisk recovery thesis.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the geopolitical tape closely — if the Strait of Hormuz situation escalates further, the AI infrastructure trade that anchors this portfolio will face another test.
